oop-intro.title: The Four Pillars of Object-Oriented Programming
oop-intro.content:
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a programming paradigm that organizes code around objects — self-contained units that bundle data and behavior together. Rather than writing a long sequence of instructions, you model your program as a collection of interacting objects.

OOP is built on four foundational pillars. Each pillar addresses a different concern in software design, and together they make code more organized, reusable, and maintainable.

The Four Pillars

Encapsulation is the practice of hiding internal implementation details and exposing only what is necessary. Objects manage their own data and protect it from unintended interference.

Inheritance allows a class to derive properties and behaviors from another class, enabling code reuse and the modeling of hierarchical relationships.

Polymorphism allows objects of different types to be treated through a shared interface. The same method call can produce different behavior depending on the object it is invoked on.

Abstraction is the practice of exposing only the essential characteristics of an object while hiding unnecessary complexity — letting programmers work with ideas rather than implementation details.

Why they matter

These pillars are not independent rules — they reinforce each other. Inheritance and polymorphism work together to build flexible class hierarchies. Encapsulation and abstraction work together to manage complexity. Mastering all four is the foundation of writing clean, scalable object-oriented code.

oop-encapsulation.title: Encapsulation
oop-encapsulation.content:
Encapsulation is the practice of bundling data and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit — the class — and restricting direct access to some of its internal components. It is one of the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming.

You don't need to know how a car engine works in order to drive. You just turn the key.

The core idea

A class exposes a public interface — the methods and properties other code is allowed to use — while keeping its internal state private. This means the internal implementation can change without breaking anything that depends on the class, as long as the public interface stays the same.

Access modifiers

In most OOP languages like C#, encapsulation is enforced using access modifiers. The most common are private, which restricts access to within the class itself, and public, which allows access from anywhere. protected falls in between, allowing access within the class and any subclass.

public class BankAccount {
    private decimal balance;

    public void Deposit(decimal amount) {
        if (amount > 0) balance += amount;
    }

    public decimal GetBalance() {
        return balance;
    }
}

Why it matters

Encapsulation protects the integrity of an object's state. It also reduces complexity — consumers of a class only need to understand its interface, not its internals. This pillar supports the KISS principle and makes code far easier to refactor safely. See also: Abstraction.

oop-inheritance.title: Inheritance
oop-inheritance.content:
Inheritance allows a class to acquire the properties and methods of another class. The class being inherited from is called the parent (or base) class, and the class that inherits is called the child (or derived) class. It is one of the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming.

Dogs inherit the general characteristics of mammals. Hunting dogs inherit from all dogs. Specific breeds inherit from their category.

The core idea

Inheritance models an is-a relationship. A Dog is an Animal. A SavingsAccount is a BankAccount. The child class gets everything the parent has, and can then extend or refine it with additional properties and methods of its own.

public class Animal {
    public string Name { get; set; }
    public void Breathe() => Console.WriteLine("Breathing...");
}

public class Dog : Animal {
    public void Fetch() => Console.WriteLine(Name + " fetches the ball!");
}

Inheritance chains

Inheritance can extend multiple levels deep. A Wolfhound could inherit from Dog, which inherits from Animal. Each level adds more specificity. However, deep inheritance chains can become difficult to maintain — it is generally advised to keep hierarchies shallow.

Why it matters

Inheritance directly supports the DRY principle (Don't Repeat Yourself). Common behavior is written once in the parent class and reused automatically in all child classes. See also: Polymorphism, which builds directly on top of inheritance.

oop-polymorphism.title: Polymorphism
oop-polymorphism.content:
Polymorphism comes from the Greek for "many shapes". It allows objects of different types to be treated through a common interface, with each object responding to the same method call in its own way. It is one of the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming.

The same message, sent to different objects, produces different behavior. That is the essence of polymorphism.

Overloading

Method overloading is when a class defines multiple methods with the same name but different parameters. The compiler routes the call to the correct version based on the arguments passed.

public class Printer {
    public void Print(string text) { ... }
    public void Print(int number) { ... }
    public void Print(string text, int copies) { ... }
}

Overriding

Method overriding is when a child class provides its own implementation of a method already defined in the parent class.

public class Animal {
    public virtual string Speak() => "...";
}
public class Dog : Animal {
    public override string Speak() => "Woof!";
}
public class Cat : Animal {
    public override string Speak() => "Meow!";
}

Interfaces

Interfaces extend polymorphism across unrelated class hierarchies. An interface defines a contract — a set of method signatures — with no implementation. Any class can implement the interface and provide its own behavior.

public interface IDrawable {
    void Draw();
}
public class Circle : IDrawable {
    public void Draw() => Console.WriteLine("Drawing a circle.");
}
public class Triangle : IDrawable {
    public void Draw() => Console.WriteLine("Drawing a triangle.");
}

See also: Inheritance.

oop-abstraction.title: Abstraction
oop-abstraction.content:
Abstraction is the process of reducing complexity by exposing only the essential features of an object and hiding everything else. It is one of the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming.

You interact with a TV through a remote control. You don't need to understand the circuitry inside to change the channel.

The core idea

Abstraction is about designing the right interface for a class. It is closely related to Encapsulation, but the distinction matters: encapsulation is the mechanism (hiding data using access modifiers), while abstraction is the design goal (hiding complexity to simplify use).

Abstract classes

public abstract class Shape {
    public abstract double Area();

    public void Describe() {
        Console.WriteLine("Area: " + Area());
    }
}

public class Circle : Shape {
    public double Radius { get; set; }
    public override double Area() => Math.PI * Radius * Radius;
}

public class Rectangle : Shape {
    public double Width { get; set; }
    public double Height { get; set; }
    public override double Area() => Width * Height;
}

Abstraction vs Encapsulation

Abstraction happens at the design level — what should this object look like from the outside? Encapsulation happens at the implementation level — how do I protect the internal state? A well-designed class achieves both.

Why it matters

Abstraction is what makes large systems manageable. When every component hides its complexity behind a clean interface, you can build, test, and reason about each piece independently. It directly supports the principle of separation of concerns.
